---
title: "Lesson 3: Multiple Parameters and Parameters in the Support of a Distribution"
subtitle: "Statistical Estimation for Data Science and AI"
---


::: {.column-margin}
{{< video 01_mles-for-multiple-and-support-parameters.mp4 >}}

[Transcript](01_mles-for-multiple-and-support-parameters.en.txt)
[Handout](02_video-slides-for-mles-for-multiple-and-support-parameters_module2_lesson3.pdf){width=700 height=980}
:::


## Maximum Likelihood Estimation: Special Cases {#sec-mle-special-cases}

This section considers two complications:

1. models with multiple unknown parameters;
2. models whose support depends on an unknown parameter.

## Multiple Parameters: The Normal Model {#sec-normal-mle-two-parameters}

::: {#exm-normal-mle-two-parameters .example}
## Normal model with unknown mean and variance

Suppose

$$
X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2),
$$

with parameter space

$$
-\infty<\mu<\infty,
\qquad
\sigma^2>0.
$$

The density of one observation is

$$
f(x;\mu,\sigma^2)
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}
\exp\left\{
-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}
\right\}.
$$
:::

The joint density is

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\boldsymbol{x};\mu,\sigma^2)
&=
\prod_{i=1}^{n}
f(x_i;\mu,\sigma^2) \\
&=
\prod_{i=1}^{n}
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}
\exp\left\{
-\frac{(x_i-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}
\right\} \\
&=
(2\pi\sigma^2)^{-n/2}
\exp\left\{
-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\mu)^2
\right\}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus the likelihood is

$$
L(\mu,\sigma^2)
=
(2\pi\sigma^2)^{-n/2}
\exp\left\{
-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\mu)^2
\right\},
$$

and the log-likelihood is

$$
\ell(\mu,\sigma^2) = -\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\mu)^2.
$$

Because there are two unknown parameters, differentiate with respect to both and solve the resulting equations simultaneously.

## Maximum Likelihood Estimator of the Mean {#sec-normal-mle-mean}

Differentiate with respect to $\mu$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial}{\partial\mu}
\ell(\mu,\sigma^2)
&=
-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}
2(x_i-\mu)(-1) \\
&=
\frac{1}{\sigma^2}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\mu).
\end{aligned}
$$

Set the derivative equal to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0
&=
\frac{1}{\sigma^2}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\mu) \\
0
&=
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\mu) \\
&=
\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i-n\mu.
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\mu
=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i
=
\overline{x}.
$$

Hence the maximum likelihood estimator is

$$
\boxed{
\hat{\mu}
=
\overline{X}
}
$$

## Maximum Likelihood Estimator of the Variance {#sec-normal-mle-variance}

Treat $\sigma^2$ as the parameter and differentiate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial}{\partial\sigma^2}
\ell(\mu,\sigma^2)
&=
-\frac{n}{2\sigma^2}
+
\frac{1}{2(\sigma^2)^2}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\mu)^2.
\end{aligned}
$$

Set the derivative equal to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0
&=
-\frac{n}{2\sigma^2}
+
\frac{1}{2(\sigma^2)^2}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\mu)^2 \\
0
&=
-n\sigma^2
+
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\mu)^2
\qquad
\text{multiply by }2(\sigma^2)^2 \\
\sigma^2
&=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\mu)^2.
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $\hat{\mu}=\overline{x}$ gives

$$
\hat{\sigma}^2
=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}
(x_i-\overline{x})^2.
$$

::: {#thm-normal-mles .theorem}
## Maximum likelihood estimators for the normal model

For

$$
X_1,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2),
$$

the maximum likelihood estimators are

$$
\boxed{
\hat{\mu}
=
\overline{X}
}
$$

and

$$
\boxed{
\hat{\sigma}^2
=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}
(X_i-\overline{X})^2
}.
$$
:::

## Sample Variance and Bias {#sec-sample-variance-bias}

Since

$$
\operatorname{Var}(X)
=
\mathbb{E}\left[(X-\mu)^2\right],
$$

a natural estimator of $\sigma^2$ is

$$
S_1^2
:=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}
(X_i-\overline{X})^2.
$$

This is exactly the maximum likelihood estimator $\hat{\sigma}^2$. However, it is biased.

To calculate its expectation, use the identity

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i-\overline{X})^2
=
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i-\mu)^2
-
n(\overline{X}-\mu)^2.
$$

Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[S_1^2]
&=
\frac{1}{n}
\mathbb{E}\left[
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i-\overline{X})^2
\right] \\
&=
\frac{1}{n}
\left\{
\sum_{i=1}^{n}
\mathbb{E}\left[(X_i-\mu)^2\right]
-
n\,
\mathbb{E}\left[(\overline{X}-\mu)^2\right]
\right\} \\
&=
\frac{1}{n}
\left\{
n\sigma^2
-
n\operatorname{Var}(\overline{X})
\right\} \\
&=
\frac{1}{n}
\left\{
n\sigma^2-\sigma^2
\right\}
\qquad
\text{since }\operatorname{Var}(\overline{X})=\frac{\sigma^2}{n} \\
&=
\frac{n-1}{n}\sigma^2.
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus an unbiased estimator is

$$
\begin{aligned}
S^2
&:=
\frac{n}{n-1}S_1^2 \\
&=
\frac{1}{n-1}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}
(X_i-\overline{X})^2.
\end{aligned}
$$

::: {#ntn-sample-variance .callout-note}
## Sample-variance convention

Unless otherwise specified, the notation $S^2$ refers to the unbiased sample variance

$$
S^2
=
\frac{1}{n-1}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}
(X_i-\overline{X})^2.
$$

The normal-model maximum likelihood estimator instead uses the denominator $n$ and is biased.
:::

## Parameters in the Support {#sec-parameters-in-support}

The second special case occurs when the unknown parameter determines the support of the distribution.

::: {#exm-uniform-support-parameter .example}
## Uniform distribution with unknown upper endpoint

Suppose

$$
X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Uniform}(0,\theta),
\qquad
\theta>0.
$$

Using an endpoint convention under which the density includes $x=\theta$,

$$
f(x;\theta)
=
\frac{1}{\theta}
\mathbf{1}_{(0,\theta]}(x).
$$
:::

The joint density is

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\boldsymbol{x};\theta)
&=
\prod_{i=1}^{n}
f(x_i;\theta) \\
&=
\frac{1}{\theta^n}
\prod_{i=1}^{n}
\mathbf{1}_{(0,\theta]}(x_i).
\end{aligned}
$$

The indicator cannot be discarded because it depends on $\theta$ and therefore forms part of the likelihood.

## Piecewise Functions, Derivatives, and Logarithms {#sec-piecewise-support-conditions}

For example, consider

$$
f(x)
=
\begin{cases}
x^2, & 0<x\leq 1, \\
x+1, & x>1.
\end{cases}
$$

Its derivative is computed within each branch:

$$
f'(x)
=
\begin{cases}
2x, & 0<x\leq 1, \\
1, & x>1.
\end{cases}
$$

The branch conditions are retained; they are not differentiated.

Likewise,

$$
\log f(x)
=
\begin{cases}
\log(x^2), & 0<x\leq 1, \\
\log(x+1), & x>1.
\end{cases}
$$

The branch conditions are retained; one does not take their logarithm.

The same principle applies to a likelihood containing an indicator: use the indicator to determine the feasible parameter values, then optimize the ordinary expression over that feasible region.

## Uniform Likelihood {#sec-uniform-likelihood}

Let

$$
x_{(n)}
=
\max(x_1,x_2,\ldots,x_n).
$$

For observed positive data,

$$
\prod_{i=1}^{n}
\mathbf{1}_{(0,\theta]}(x_i)
=
\mathbf{1}_{[x_{(n)},\infty)}(\theta).
$$

Therefore the likelihood can be written as

$$
L(\theta)
=
\frac{1}{\theta^n}
\mathbf{1}_{[x_{(n)},\infty)}(\theta).
$$

The indicator imposes the constraint

$$
\theta\geq x_{(n)}.
$$

Within this feasible region,

$$
L(\theta)
=
\theta^{-n},
$$

which is strictly decreasing in $\theta$. The likelihood is therefore maximized by choosing the smallest feasible value of $\theta$:

$$
\hat{\theta}
=
x_{(n)}.
$$

::: {#thm-uniform-endpoint-mle .theorem}
## Maximum likelihood estimator of a uniform endpoint

For

$$
X_1,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Uniform}(0,\theta),
$$

the maximum likelihood estimator of the upper endpoint is

$$
\boxed{
\hat{\theta}
=
X_{(n)}
=
\max(X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n)
}.
$$
:::

::: {.callout-note}
The endpoint convention does not change the probability distribution because a single endpoint has probability zero. Writing the support as $(0,\theta]$ makes the likelihood maximum attained at $\theta=X_{(n)}$.
:::

## Reflection

So one point that came up is that we might have to solve simultaneous equations to find the MLE when there are multiple parameters. 

The multiple equations might not be linear which would make MLE non-trivial to compute theoretically.

When the parameter is part of the support of the distribution we can get a Max on the Data. And Max is not differentiable.

There are also some distributions with three parameters like the generalized Pareto distribution, Weibull distribution, Log-normal distribution, and others. These would presumably require solving three equations simultaneously to find the MLEs.

But in practice we might want to use a simulation based approach to find the MLEs in these more complex cases...

Another issue that will soon come up is that MLE are asymptotically unbiased. I.E. they get better as sample size increases. Which suggests that the data has an important role to play in the quality of the MLE.

So in practical terms one would like to estimate MLE from data and to estimate is an estimator is Unbiased and or asymptotically unbiased.
